coredata.dc:title: A novel ratiometric fluorescent probe for detection of l-glutamic acid based on dual-emission carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Lin
Liu, Lizhen
Bai, Yunfeng
Qin, Jun
Chen, Meng
Feng, Feng
coredata.dc:description: In this article, we report for the first time the use of a dual-emission carbon dots (CDs) with orange-yellow fluorescence for the detection of l-glutamic acid (L-Glu). The CDs was synthesized through a facile strategy of one-pot hydrothermal route using o-phenylenediamine (oPD) and oxalic acid. The CDs exhibit two fluorescence emission peaks around 453 nm and 560 nm when the excitation wavelength is at 390 nm. In the existence of L-Glu the fluorescence at 560 nm was decreased, whereas the fluorescence at 453 nm was constant. The fluorescence intensity ratio at 560 nm and 453 nm (F560/F453) expressed two great linear relationships in the L-Glu concentration range from 0 to 200 μM and 200–400 μM, respectively, with a detection limit (LOD) of about 0.085 μM. In addition, it was used to analyze L-Glu in fetal bovine serum samples successfully, which recoveries were ranging from 97.07 to 103.7%. Those results demonstrate CDs can be further explored in biomedicine studies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
l-glutamic acid
Ratiometric detection
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratiometric detection of L-Glu using CDs
Determination of L-Glu in fetal bovine serum

Agricultural Key Research and Development Plan Project Fund of Datong City

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A)TEM image of CDs and the upper right corner is a HRTEM image with lattice fringes (B) size distribution of CDs.
XPS spectra of CDs (A) and high resolution spectrum of C1s(B), N1s(C), O1s(D).

(A) UV–vis spectra (black), optimum excitation and emission spectrogram of CDs. The photograph of CDs solution under natural light (left) and below UV light (right) on the inset. (B)The emission spectrum of CDs at diverse excitation wavelengths (360–450 nm).
(A) Fluorescence spectrogram of CDs at diverse concentrations of L-Glu (0–400 μM). (B) and (C) are the linear relationships with the F560/F453 and concentration of L-Glu.
(A)The F560/F453 of CDs solution with various interfering substances. (B) The F560/F453 of CDs—L-Glu system before and after commixing diverse interfering material (n = 3).
Schematic diagram of detection of glutamic acid by dual-emission CDs.
Determination results of L-Glu in fetal bovine serum.

head.ce:title: A novel ratiometric fluorescent probe for detection of l-glutamic acid based on dual-emission carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this article, we report for the first time the use of a dual-emission carbon dots (CDs) with orange-yellow fluorescence for the detection of l-glutamic acid (L-Glu). The CDs was synthesized through a facile strategy of one-pot hydrothermal route using o-phenylenediamine (oPD) and oxalic acid. The CDs exhibit two fluorescence emission peaks around 453 nm and 560 nm when the excitation wavelength is at 390 nm. In the existence of L-Glu the fluorescence at 560 nm was decreased, whereas the fluorescence at 453 nm was constant. The fluorescence intensity ratio at 560 nm and 453 nm (F560/F453) expressed two great linear relationships in the L-Glu concentration range from 0 to 200 μM and 200–400 μM, respectively, with a detection limit (LOD) of about 0.085 μM. In addition, it was used to analyze L-Glu in fetal bovine serum samples successfully, which recoveries were ranging from 97.07 to 103.7%. Those results demonstrate CDs can be further explored in biomedicine studies.

ce:section.ce:para: l-glutamic acid (L-Glu, Fig. S1), an acidic amino acid, is the main ingredient of protein and one of the essential amino acids of nitrogen metabolism in organisms, which is of great significance in metabolism. Although L-Glu is a non-essential amino acid in humans [1], it plays a crucial function in controlling and regulating the central and peripheral nervous systems [2]. Simultaneously, L-Glu is also important in human learning, memory, and brain function [3]. However, a lot of neurological or psychiatric illnesses, such as Alzheimer's and Parkinson's [4], would occur when the body does not have normal levels of L-Glu. In the food industry, L-Glu also plays an essential role. Due to its unique fresh taste, L-Glu is commonly utilized as a flavor enhancer in chicken essence, monosodium glutamate, soy sauce, and some condiments. Nevertheless, when people take too much L-Glu, it can cause allergic reactions, such as headaches and stomach pains [5]. Consequently, it's essential to constitute a green, uncomplicated, sensitive, rapid, and low-cost analytical way for L-Glu detection in the foodstuff and biological samples.

In recent years, various methods such as capillary electrophoresis-mass spectrometry [6], raman spectroscopy [7], liquid chromatography [2], and electrochemical biosensor [8,9] have been developed and used for the detection of L-Glu. And most of these methods require expensive instruments, professional operators, complex sample pre-treatment, and a lot of operating time. Miniature chemical sensors are of interest to researchers because of their simplicity, portability, and low cost. Among them are fluorescent sensors, which are widely used for trace detection due to their fast response, high sensitivity, and good selectivity [10,11].

Carbon dots (CDs), being a shining star in the carbon nanomaterial household, have the advantages of low expense, easy synthesis, excellent biocompatibility, and stable structure. Owing to these excellent qualities, CDs have attracted wonderful notice in the fields of latent fingerprint identification [12], photoelectric [13], optical imaging [14], regulating plant's biochemical processes [15], chemical sensing [16], and theranostic nanomedicine [17]. Although CDs have many significant advantages, most of the CDs reported so far are single wavelengths, and the results were easily influenced by the environment, light source, probe concentration, and instrumentation [18]. To overcome these drawbacks, researchers have combined CDs with other fluorescent nanomaterials to construct ratiometric fluorescent sensors, such as Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOF) [19–21], lanthanide ions [22], organic dyes [23], gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) [24,25]. These ratiometric probes with dual emission peaks significantly improve the efficiency and accuracy of biological analysis [26]. For example, Lesani [27] and coworkers have established a ratiometric sensor to detect Fe3+ by coupling fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) molecules to CDs. Fe3+ simultaneously quenched the fluorescence of CDs and FITC molecules, with a remarkable LOD of 2.21 μM. Xu [28] and others encapsulated the CDs in MOF to achieve signal amplification for highly sensitive detection of quercetin. It also realized the detection of trace amounts of quercetin in real samples. Zhang [29] group prepared ratiometric fluorescent probes by coupling CDs with AuNCs wrapped in bovine serum albumin. The purple fluorescence of this probe was quenched by dopamine. This is because dopamine prevents the förster resonant energy transfer (FRET) process between CDs and AuNCs, which causes a change in fluorescence intensity. These ratiometric fluorescent probes with self-calibration exhibit higher sensitivity in detection. So far, most CDs-based ratiometric probes have been assembled with fluorescent materials by physical or chemical methods [30], and these processes are tedious. Therefore, it remains a great desire to explore more and more CDs that emit double fluorescence at a single excitation wavelength.

In the current work, a new dual-emission CDs for L-Glu detection was explored via a one-step hydrothermal manner utilizing o-phenylenediamine (oPD) and oxalic acid. As shown in Scheme 1 , while the excitation wavelength is at 390 nm, there are two emission peaks at 453 nm and 560 nm, respectively. With L-Glu added, the fluorescence intensity of emission peak at 560 nm has been quenched obviously, while the intensity of the emission peak at 453 nm did not change significantly. In addition, CDs was successfully used to analyze L-Glu in the fetal bovine serum samples. To our knowledge, this is the initially reported detection of L-Glu using dual-emission CDs.

Aladdin Co., Ltd provided dl-Homocysteine (DL-Hom), phenylalanine (Phe), alanine (Ala), methionine (Met), leucine (Leu), tryptophan (Trp), threonine (Thr), and glycine (Gly). o-Phenylenediamin (oPD) was entertained from Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory Co., Ltd. ZnCl2, MgCl2, NaCl, KCl, BaCl2, CoCl2, CaCl2, MnCl2, l-glutamic acid (L-Glu), and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were bought from Macklin Co., Ltd. All reagents utilized were no less than analytically pure. Deionized water (18.25 MΩ) was applied during all works.

F-2500 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan) was applied to obtain fluorescence spectra. UV–vis spectrogram was measured through a Lambda 35 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was accomplished by a Tecnai F30 microscope (FEI, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) analysis were examined through ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fischer, USA). Used Nicolet iS5 spectrometer (Thermo Fischer, USA) to performed transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis. Fluorescence lifetime analysis were obtained from a QM8000 spectrofluorometer (Horiba Scientific, Japan).

The CDs were prepared utilizing a one-pot hydrothermal way following a formerly described method with several alterations [31,32]. In particular, disperse 0.5 g of oPD and 0.3 g of oxalic acid in 20 mL of deionized water for 10 min under ultrasonic conditions. Afterward, the consistent solution was moved into a 50 mL teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. After unconditioned cooling, the rough products were standing for 5 days at 4 °C. Then, the crude products were filtered to take impurities off. Ultimately, the CDs powder was gathered through freeze drying and deposited at 4 °C for characterization and further study.

The measurements of L-Glu were performed in deionized water. In brief, varying bulks of L-Glu solution and CDs solution of 20 μL (10 mg mL−1) were mixed with 2980 μL deionized water. The corresponding emission spectra were carried out at the excitation wavelength of 390 nm. Then the relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio at 560 nm and 453 nm (F560/F453) and concentration of L-Glu was established.

The specificity of the dual-emission CDs was assessed by adding other interference in place of L-Glu. Those interfere included metal ions, such as Ba2+, K+, Mg2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, and Na+, amino acids like dl-Homocysteine (DL-Hom), phenylalanine (Phe), alanine (Ala), methionine (Met), leucine (Leu), tryptophan (Trp), threonine (Thr), and glycine (Gly). In anti-interference experiments, L-Glu was added after other interfering substances to explore its anti-interference performance. In the experiments, the concentrations of L-Glu is 200 μM and the volume of other interfering substances is 600 μM.

Fresh fetal bovine serum was acquired from Macklin Co., Ltd and pretreated for further use. The fetal bovine serum samples were diluted 100 times using deionized water, then stored in the refrigerator at −4 °C. Different concentrations of L-Glu were added to prepare the spiked samples.

The morphology and size of CDs were revealed through TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A, CDs display homogeneous spherical and great dispersion. The representative highresolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of the CDs in the inset of Fig. 1A demonstrates that the lattice spacing of the CDs is 0.23 nm.Fig. 1B exhibits that the size of CDs distribution is in the 1.45–5.95 nm range and the average size of approximately 3.29 nm.

FTIR spectra was gauged to study the functional groups on the surface of CDs (Fig. S2). The typical absorption peaks of CDs occurs at around the 3377 cm−1 (O–H stretching vibration), 3192 cm−1 (N–H stretching vibration), 3033 cm−1 (C–H stretching vibration), 1631 cm−1 (CO stretching vibration), 1500 cm−1 (CC stretching vibration), 1395 cm−1 (CN stretching vibration), 1263 cm−1 (C–N stretching vibration), 1138 cm−1 (C–O stretching vibration), and 821 cm−1 (N–H bending vibration). Mentioned FTIR result suggests that the CDs own –NH2, –OH, and –COOH functional groups on the surface.

XPS, a powerful tool to study element compositions and surface states of CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 A, there are three typical peaks in the full XPS spectrum of CDs, which are attributed to C1s (284.8 eV), N1s (399.6 eV), as well as O1s (531.7 eV), respectively. In addition, the atomic percentage of C, N, and O, is 69%, 19.6%, and 11.4%, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2B) embodied three principal peaks at 284.8 eV (C–C/CC), 285.91 eV (C–N), and 287.8 eV (CO), respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) appeared three bonds at 399.22 eV (C–NC), 399.65 eV (C–N–C), and 400.76 eV (N–H), respectively. The oxygen (Fig. 2D) of CDs has also displayed three typical peaks at 531.35 eV (CO), 532.3 eV (C–O–O/C–OH), and 534.65eV (O–H), respectively. These consequences are consistent with the FTIR results. Meanwhile, the numerous hydrophilic groups on the surface of the CDs provide the basis for its excellent water solubility.

Then we investigated the optical characteristics of the CDs, such as UV–vis absorption, fluorescence spectrum, and quantum yield (QY). In the UV–vis spectra (Fig. 3 A), two absorption peaks were observed around 232 nm and 289 nm (black curve), which come from the π—π* transition of CC bond and n—π* transition of CO band, respectively. Fig. 3A shows that CDs solution is yellow in visible light but showed an orange-yellow color under UV light at 365 nm. In addition to this information, we can observe that the optimum excitation and emission wavelengths of the CDs appeared at 416 nm (red curve) and 560 nm (blue curve), respectively.

Furthermore, fluorescence spectra were investigated in detail by varying the excitation wavelength from 360 nm to 450 nm. As shown in Fig. 3B, there are double emission peaks at approximately 400 nm and 560 nm, and the intensity of the emission peaks changes with the excitation wavelength. As the excitation wavelength was increased from 360 nm to 450 nm, one of the emission peaks near 400 nm is red-shifted and accompanied by a weakening of this intensity, while the emission peak at 560 nm has no change in position but only in intensity. Considering the emission intensities of two peaks, we finally chose 390 nm as the excitation wavelength for succeeding experimentation, and the emission peaks appeared at 453 nm and 560 nm, respectively. In addition, the absolute fluorescence QY of the CDs was about 16.4%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric detection of L-Glu using CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, all operations were accomplished at room temperature. To improve the accuracy of the experiment, it needs to be done quickly and preferably within 1 h. As the L-Glu concentration increased from 0 μM to 400 μM, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 560 nm displayed an apparent decrease and fluorescence intensity at 453 nm was kept constant (Fig. 4 A). This result suggests that the CDs have an excellent response to L-Glu. Fig. 4B and C shows the relationship between the concentration of L-Glu and the F560/F453 of CDs, which exhibited two good linear correlations in the range of 0–200 μM and 200–400 μM, respectively. The linear regression equations are F560/F453 = −0.0126 [C]+5.6017 (R2 = 0.9931) and F560/F453 = −0.0065 [C]+4.4099 (R2 = 0.9961), respectively, where [C] is the concentrations of L-Glu. The LOD was calculated to be 0.085 μM and 0.099 μM respectively, using the following equation 3σ/k（the ratio of the standard deviation of the blank solution (n = 10) to the slope of the calibration curve）.

These data illustrate the great potentiality of dual-emission CDs for the detection of L-Glu. Moreover, we compared our method with the several reported L-Glu assays (Table S1) and found that this dual-emission CDs has a wider linear range and a lower LOD. Although the LOD is still not at the level of liquid chromatography, it has great advantages in terms of simplicity of operation and time consumption.

To investigate the sensing mechanism of CDs to L-Glu, a series of experiments have been implemented. Fig. S3A shows UV-Via absorption spectra of L-Glu and fluorescence spectra of CDs. It is clear that the absorption spectrum of L-Glu did not overlap with the excitation and emission spectra of CDs. Consequently, the interaction between CDs and L-Glu is neither resulted from inner filter effect (IFE) nor förster resonant energy transfer (FRET) [33]. In addition, average fluorescence lifetime of CDs solution existence L-Glu (τ1) was 2.0 ns and non-existence L-Glu (τ0) was 2.1 ns (Fig. S3B), respectively, meaning the fluorescence lifetime did not change with the addition of L-Glu. Since dynamic quenching effect (DQE) causes changes in fluorescence lifetime while static quenching process (SQE) do not [34], we presume that it is a SQE between CDs and L-Glu. The UV-Via absorption spectra of CDs, L-Glu, and CDs—L-Glu also provide evidence for our conjecture. As shown in Fig. S3C, the absorbance at 205 nm disappeared and the absorbance that appeared at 232 nm and 289 nm changed significantly with the addition of L-Glu to the CDs solution (red), which can infer there was generated ground-state complex in the CDs—L-Glu system. Furthermore, both SQD and DQE were following stern-volmer equation:
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity proportion of CDs in the non-existence [(F560/F453)0] and the existence of L-Glu (F560/F453). KSV is the stern-volmer constant, and [Q] is the concentration of L-Glu. [(F560/F453)0]/(F560/F453) and the concentration of L-Glu display a good linear correlation (Fig. S3D), with KSV = 3.9 × 103 L mol−1 and R2 = 0.9918.

To further learn the quenching mechanism, the related Kq (the rate constant of quenching) can be calculated using the equation:
K
S V
=
K
q
τ 0

To calculate the Kq = 1.86 × 1012 L mol−1 s−1, which is much larger than the dynamic quenching constant (2 × 1010 L mol−1s−1). Assuring the formation between CDs and L-Glu should be caused by SQE [35].

Fine selectivity is a key parameter for feasibility analysis. To research the selectivity of the CDs, diverse potentially interfering substances were added to the CDs solution, including Ba2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Na+, DL-Hom, Phe, Ala, Met, Leu, Trp, Th, and Cly. As shown in Fig. 5 A, only L-Glu apparently quenches the fluorescence of CDs when the intervening substances have an unspectacular effect on the system.From the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence of CDs quenched by L-Glu via SQE. In order to confirm the SQE only exists between the CDs and L-Glu, several UV-vis absorption spectra of interferences and CDs are displayed in Fig. S4. It is obvious that the UV-vis absorption peak of CDs changes only in the presence of L-Glu, and other possible disturbance species show inconspicuous effects. These results indicate that the SQE is only between CDs and L-Glu.

When the CDs—L-Glu sensing system and the competing substances coexist, even if the concentration of the interfering substances is three times that of the L-Glu, the ratio of F560/F453 did not show obvious changes (Fig. 5B). Therefore, the prepared CDs could take for a ratiometric fluorescence probe for L-Glu detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of L-Glu in fetal bovine serum
ce:section.ce:para: To test the possibility of the mentioned method, we utilized the dual-emission CDs to detect L-Glu in fetal bovine serum samples. Different concentrations of L-Glu (50 μM, 100 μM and, 150 μM) were added to the samples, and Table 1 displays the analytical results. The recoveries of three samples were in the range of 97.07–103.7%, with RSD (n = 3) of 2.07–4.52%. The above consequences confirmed that this dual-emission CDs was feasible and accurate for sensing L-Glu in fetal bovine serum samples.

In summary, a novel dual-emission CDs for L-Glu was successfully developed using oPD and oxalic acid. The obtained CDs exhibited dual fluorescence emission at 453 nm and 560 nm, respectively. L-Glu can notably quench the fluorescence at 560 nm while the emission peak at 453 nm was essentially unchanged. The CDs was developed as an efficient platform for ratiometric L-Glu sensing by monitoring the change of F560/F453, with two linear ranges (0–200 μM, 200–400 μM) and high sensitivity (LOD = 0.085 μM). The developed CDs could not only test L-Glu in aqueous solutions but also display sensitive responses toward L-Glu in fetal bovine serums. These results prove its potential applicability in the biological field.

Lin Yuan: Methodology, Visualization, Data curation, Writing. Lizhen Li: Guidance, Verification, Supervision, Review, Editing. Yunfeng Bai: Investigation. Jun Qin: Investigation. Meng Chen:Investigation. Feng Feng: Investigation, Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge for financial support from Applied Basic Research Project Fund of Shanxi Province (201901D211434) and Agricultural Key Research and Development Plan Project Fund of Datong City (No. 2021053 and 2021054) and Graduate Education Innovation Project of Shanxi Datong University (21CX03).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2022.123416.
